claims.claim_text: The CKA exam tests workload autoscaling skills, including configuring workload autoscaling.
claims.span_hint: line 36 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: The Linux Foundation CKA page explicitly lists workload autoscaling in the exam scope.

claims.claim_text: HPA runs its control loop every 15 seconds by default, using the sync-period setting, and averages metrics across pods for utilization targets.
claims.rationale: The HPA docs state the default sync period is 15 seconds and describe averaging across pods for targetAverage metrics.

claims.claim_text: By default, HPA uses a 5 minute downscale stabilization window to reduce flapping.
claims.rationale: The HPA docs document the default downscale stabilization window as 300 seconds (5 minutes).

claims.claim_text: The module says HPA cannot function without metrics-server installed in the cluster.
claims.anchor_text: HPA can't function without metrics-server installed in the cluster. It provides the CPU/memory metrics that HPA needs.
claims.suggested_rewrite: CPU- and memory-based HPA relies on the resource metrics pipeline, typically provided by metrics-server. Custom or external metric scaling also requires the corresponding adapter API.
claims.rationale: The blanket claim is too broad because HPA can scale on custom or external metrics without metrics-server.

claims.claim_text: With Kubernetes 1.35 era in-place resize support, VPA can use in-place resource updates for CPU and memory in the appropriate mode.
claims.rationale: The VPA docs describe the InPlaceOrRecreate mode, and the resize docs describe in-place CPU and memory resize as stable in v1.35.

claims.claim_text: HPA needs metrics-server or an equivalent resource metrics pipeline to read CPU and memory usage.
claims.rationale: The HPA and metrics pipeline docs explain that resource-metric HPA uses metrics.k8s.io, commonly provided by metrics-server.

claims.claim_text: HPA computes desired replicas with ceil(currentReplicas * currentMetricValue / desiredMetricValue).
claims.rationale: The HPA algorithm docs give this formula explicitly.

claims.claim_text: By default, HPA scale-up behavior allows up to 4 pods or 100% of current replicas every 15 seconds, and the behavior field customizes this.
claims.rationale: The default behavior section in the HPA docs shows these default policies and the behavior field.

claims.claim_text: Scaling on custom metrics requires a custom metrics adapter, such as prometheus-adapter, serving the custom.metrics.k8s.io API.
claims.rationale: The HPA docs require custom.metrics.k8s.io for custom metrics, and the Prometheus Adapter repo is the canonical example in the section pool.

claims.claim_text: VPA adjusts CPU and memory requests and limits based on observed usage, whereas HPA scales by replica count.
claims.span_hint: line 206 | section: Part 3: Vertical Pod Autoscaler
claims.rationale: The VPA docs define vertical autoscaling as adjusting resource requests and limits instead of adding pods.

claims.claim_text: The table says VPA Auto mode uses in-place resize on Kubernetes 1.35+ and falls back to recreate.
claims.anchor_text: | `Auto` | Uses in-place resize (K8s 1.35+) when possible, falls back to recreate |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | `InPlaceOrRecreate` | Attempts in-place resource updates when possible, and falls back to recreate when needed |
claims.rationale: Current VPA docs say `Auto` is deprecated and behaves like `Recreate`; in-place behavior belongs to `InPlaceOrRecreate`.

claims.claim_text: The note says VPA Auto mode can adjust running pods without restart in Kubernetes 1.35.
claims.anchor_text: With in-place pod resize GA, VPA in `Auto` mode can now adjust CPU and memory on running pods without restart — a major improvement for stateful workloads.
claims.suggested_rewrite: With in-place pod resize available in Kubernetes 1.35, VPA can use `InPlaceOrRecreate` to try in-place CPU and memory updates and fall back to recreation when needed.
claims.rationale: The upstream VPA docs distinguish deprecated `Auto` from `InPlaceOrRecreate`.

claims.claim_text: If HPA cannot fetch the underlying resource metrics, it cannot scale on that metric.
claims.span_hint: lines 254-255 and line 268 | sections: Common Mistakes, Quiz 1 answer
claims.rationale: The HPA and metrics pipeline docs explain that scaling depends on access to the metrics APIs.

claims.claim_text: Without CPU resource requests on pods, HPA cannot calculate utilization percentage for CPU-based autoscaling.
claims.span_hint: lines 255-255 and line 268 | sections: Common Mistakes, Quiz 1 answer
claims.rationale: The HPA docs say resource requests must be set to compute utilization, and the resource management docs define requests.

claims.claim_text: After fixing metrics-server and CPU requests, the HPA should show utilization within 15-30 seconds.
claims.anchor_text: After both fixes, the HPA should show actual utilization within 15-30 seconds and begin making scaling decisions.
claims.suggested_rewrite: After both fixes, the HPA should begin reporting utilization once fresh metrics have been collected and the control loop has run, typically within a short interval.
claims.rationale: The exact 15-30 second timing is environment-dependent, but the general lesson about metrics collection plus the HPA control loop is well supported.

claims.claim_text: If HPA creates pods that cannot be scheduled on existing nodes, node autoscaling can add nodes to make them schedulable.
claims.rationale: The node autoscaling docs explain that unschedulable pods can trigger provisioning of new nodes.

claims.claim_text: Custom metrics such as packets-per-second respond faster to traffic changes than CPU utilization.
claims.anchor_text: Also consider using HPA with custom metrics (like the `packets-per-second` metric shown earlier) instead of CPU, which responds faster to traffic changes than CPU utilization does.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Also consider using HPA with custom metrics (like the `packets-per-second` metric shown earlier) when CPU utilization is not the best early signal for incoming demand.
claims.rationale: The exact responsiveness comparison is workload-specific, but using custom metrics to scale on application-level demand is well supported.

claims.claim_text: The quiz answer says VPA Auto mode on Kubernetes 1.35+ uses in-place pod resize without restarting the container.
claims.anchor_text: Once you trust the recommendations, switch to `updateMode: "Auto"` which, on Kubernetes 1.35+, uses in-place pod resize to adjust resources without restarting the container -- critical for a cache that would lose data on restart.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Once you trust the recommendations, switch to `updateMode: "InPlaceOrRecreate"` if you want VPA to attempt in-place resource updates and fall back to recreation when needed.
claims.rationale: Upstream VPA docs say `Auto` is deprecated and equivalent to `Recreate`; in-place behavior is documented under `InPlaceOrRecreate`.

claims.claim_text: VPA should not be used with HPA on CPU or memory; if combined, HPA should use custom or external metrics instead.
claims.span_hint: lines 258-259 and lines 286-286 | sections: Common Mistakes, Quiz 4 answer
claims.rationale: The autoscaler issue reproduces the documented VPA limitation verbatim, and the section pool includes the canonical custom-metrics adapter example.

further_reading.title: Horizontal Pod Autoscaling
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary reference for HPA behavior, algorithm details, metrics APIs, and scaling policies.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Metrics Server
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains the resource metrics pipeline that backs kubectl top and CPU/memory-based autoscaling.

further_reading.title: Vertical Pod Autoscaling
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers VPA architecture, update modes, and current guidance on in-place versus recreate-based updates.

notes: The main doc drift is in the VPA section: current upstream docs distinguish `InPlaceOrRecreate` from deprecated `Auto`, while the module currently treats `Auto` as the in-place mode.
